# RFC-0005: Evidence And Operator Journal Contract

> **Version:** 0.12.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** spec

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0005:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0005c-summary"></a>

This RFC governs durable evidence: readable file-first records whose immutable `resolved` object is shared by dry-run and execution, process-keyed runtime evidence, unredacted local values, and the operator scratchpad journal on the same time axis. Server and image workflows are defined by [RFC-0003](../rfc/RFC-0003.md) and [RFC-0007](../rfc/RFC-0007.md). [RFC-0001](../rfc/RFC-0001.md) holds the specification overview and authority map.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0005:C-EVIDENCE] Records And Evidence (Normative) <a id="rfc-0005c-evidence"></a>

InferLab MUST create a durable local execution record after resolution is frozen and before the first state-affecting external command of a non-dry-run server, recipe, manual Bench, or image workflow.

Record directory identifiers MUST be readable and use these shapes when the corresponding fact exists:

- `<UTC>-serve-<server>[-<case>]-<pid>`;
- `<UTC>-recipe-<recipe>[-<case>]-<pid>`;
- `<UTC>-bench-<bench>-<pid>`;
- `<UTC>-image-<image>-<pid>`.

A server record directory MUST NOT include one role name because one record may own several roles. Per-process log filenames MUST include the process identity.

Every server or recipe record MUST contain one immutable `resolved` object produced by [RFC-0003:C-RESOLUTION](../rfc/RFC-0003.md#rfc-0003c-resolution). Dry-run MUST present the same resolved shape without claiming execution. Runtime code MUST NOT mutate or reconstruct that object after freezing it.

A direct `serve start` record MUST preserve a null recipe identity. A recipe record MUST preserve recipe, workload suite, server, and selected case identities. A server with base behavior and no selected case MUST preserve a null case identity.

The resolved server evidence MUST preserve producing InferLab version, record schema, workspace and dirty-source identity, stack and realization identity, model and served name, topology, selected case and selection source, typed effective behavior, ordered behavior declarations, explicit overrides, placement and its selection source, integration and protocol identities, planning and rendering request and response digests, role links, routing ownership and policy, server-control capabilities, public endpoint, and cleanup plan when those facts apply.

The resolved server topology MUST be hierarchical: canonical role, then replica, then rank. It MUST include every model-serving role and the canonical router when one exists. It MUST NOT duplicate the same allocations in a second flat process list.

Each resolved role MUST preserve role identity, declared and effective replica counts, declared and complete effective settings, declared and complete effective parallelism, whole-replica device count, readiness and endpoint contracts, and profiling capability or preparation.

The ordered behavior declarations MUST be the sole provenance for server, selected-case, and invocation inputs. Each declaration MUST identify its source and preserve its common and role-scoped inputs. Resolved effective fields MUST NOT duplicate that information in per-field source values or source maps; integration defaults and adjustments MUST remain observable from the declared and effective values.

Each resolved replica MUST preserve its role-local identity, routing or serving-entry semantics, and ordered ranks.

Each resolved rank MUST preserve process identity, rank index and count, machine, ordered devices, optional model locator and whether it came from the machine-specific map or fallback, endpoint, allocated ports, runtime-cache inputs and path, launch context, dependencies, exact command and environment, launch files, readiness, and container identity when those facts apply. A router rank MUST preserve its empty devices and absent model locator.

Runtime evidence MUST be stored separately from resolved allocation and keyed by process identity. It MUST preserve logs, durable runtime handles, spawn and readiness outcomes, hardware observations, control actions, failures, termination, and cleanup. It MUST NOT repeat machine, device, locator, endpoint, command, or dependency facts already authoritative in the resolved rank.

A long-running server record MUST preserve its lifecycle state until stop or recovery finalizes it. Server status, logs, stop, and recovery MUST be executable from the record without reloading workspace or local configuration.

For every machine that hosts a serving process, execution evidence MUST preserve the hardware actually assigned at launch: each assigned device's model name, memory capacity, and device UUID, plus driver version. The probe MUST run through the same launch path as serving processes. A failed probe MUST fail before serving process launch naming machine and error.

Profiling evidence MUST additionally preserve capture targets and their role/replica/rank bindings, profiler identities, storage, capture plans, semantic windows, range mappings, control actions, collection finalization, report references, verification, and failures.

Eval evidence MUST additionally preserve execution kind, effective request, result metrics, raw artifacts, failure, and skip reason. Built-in OpenAI smoke evidence MUST identify its kind and InferLab version and MUST NOT claim that lm-eval or a Bench runtime participated. lm-eval evidence MUST preserve host platform, toolchain manifest and lock, runtime and runner identities, invocation, logs, and process outcome.

When a recipe applies measurement overrides, each measurement child record MUST preserve the declared definition, complete effective definition, and ordered override provenance.

Bench evidence MUST preserve host platform, toolchain manifest and lock, AIPerf and runner identities, adaptive and normalization policy identities, exact cases, resolved AIPerf configuration, native invocation, request counts, normalized metrics, raw artifacts, logs, process and cleanup outcomes, and failures.

A workload record MUST carry exactly one tagged evidence variant, `eval` or `bench`, as its sole workload-kind authority. The Eval variant MUST contain only Eval cases and Eval-only result evidence; the Bench variant MUST contain only Bench cases, Bench request-source evidence, and the optional adaptive summary. A case MUST keep process, timing, paths, native invocation, raw artifacts, and terminal error as common evidence while storing Eval gate, trial, task-metric, and native Eval outcome fields only in Eval evidence, and load, request-count, normalization, population-slice, prefix-cache-reset, and SLO fields only in Bench evidence.

A failed or skipped case MUST omit a normalization schema and other result-derived fields unless an accepted runner result established those values. An empty placeholder MUST NOT claim that a result schema, request count, or normalized result existed.

Workload record schema version 7 introduces this tagged evidence shape. Typed workload-record readers MUST reject earlier schema versions rather than infer case kind from legacy optional-field combinations. Summary-only readers MAY continue reading the legacy top-level `kind`, `definition_id`, and `status` fields without interpreting legacy cases; InferLab MUST NOT rewrite or silently coerce legacy mixed case records into the tagged shape.

A manual Bench record MUST preserve the target server record, its producer version and serving snapshot, and the invoking InferLab version and measurement workspace snapshot that supplied the Bench and overrides.

Image records MUST preserve the immutable resolved image plan, build inputs, native builder commands, content closure, package and assembly evidence, immutable image identity, inspection and export outcomes, stack check results, validation references and outcomes, partial failures, and cleanup under [RFC-0007:C-IMAGE-BUILD](../rfc/RFC-0007.md#rfc-0007c-image-build).

Dry-run and records MUST preserve actual effective model locators, machine hosts, endpoints and ports, device assignments, command arguments, environment values, and local paths when those facts apply.

InferLab MUST NOT perform value-level redaction or maintain a separate shareable record projection. Operators are responsible for access control, retention, and sharing of local records.

A dirty-workspace record MUST preserve baseline revision, dirty marker, effective source-content digest, and non-reproducible status. It MUST NOT claim that the baseline revision alone reproduces execution.

A finalized record MUST remain inspectable without the original workspace, original machines, or rerunning the operation. InferLab records need not remain compatible with record schemas or directory layouts predating this contract.

**Rationale:** The immutable resolved object is the authoritative statement of intended execution, while process-keyed evidence reports what happened. The role → replica → rank hierarchy makes heterogeneous P/D and zero-device routers readable without duplicating allocation facts, and readable record identifiers answer which named workflow ran without pretending a multi-role record belongs to one role.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0005:C-SCRATCHPAD-JOURNAL] Operator Scratchpad Journal (Normative) <a id="rfc-0005c-scratchpad-journal"></a>

InferLab MUST provide an operator scratchpad journal: one workspace-local append-only stream of entries per workspace, stored as machine state under the workspace state directory.

A journal entry MUST identify its creation timestamp, its author, its free text, an optional topic label, and zero or more referenced execution records. Entries MUST be appended in creation order, and the scratchpad surface MUST NOT edit or delete an appended entry.

Entry timestamps MUST use the same UTC format as execution record identifiers so entries and records order on one time axis.

Appending MUST require no prior setup: the journal and any topic exist by first use, a topic carries no lifecycle state, and no workspace-level mode routes entries — an entry's topic is stated on the entry itself or absent. Entries without a topic form the common stream.

Appending an entry MUST validate that each referenced execution record exists and MUST NOT mutate a referenced record.

The journal rendering MUST present entries chronologically with a one-line summary for each referenced record and MUST support restricting the view to one topic. A default view MAY limit itself to recent entries, but it MUST state how many earlier entries are omitted, and a complete view MUST be available.

Scratchpad state MUST be excluded from workspace source identity and MUST NOT become an input to resolution, execution, or record content.

Scratchpad state MUST remain inspectable as plain files without the InferLab binary.

**Rationale:** Journal writes compete with the experiment itself, so the write path carries no ceremony and no mode. A sticky active-thread designation is only convenient when narratives do not interleave — exactly when routing is unneeded — and silently misroutes append-only entries when several efforts (for example, multiple models validated side by side in one workspace) do interleave; an explicit per-entry topic is the only routing that cannot land an entry in the wrong narrative, and an absent topic costs nothing. Reading is rare but resume-oriented, so the default view leads with the recent tail. Entries share the record-identifier time axis so records and narrative interleave into one history without a second authority: records own execution facts, the journal owns the narrative, and neither feeds resolution. Scratchpad state stays out of source identity for the same reason records do, and one line per entry under a short exclusive file lock — the timestamp taken inside the lock — keeps concurrent entries whole and in time-axis order on any filesystem, including network mounts where bare O_APPEND guarantees neither; readers take the shared lock, so a partially written line is never observed.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0005:C-LM-EVAL-EVIDENCE] lm-eval Execution Evidence (Normative) <a id="rfc-0005c-lm-eval-evidence"></a>

lm-eval evidence MUST preserve the declared and resolved task-source kind; the built-in task name, bundled task name and bundled task-closure digest, or the workspace-relative and resolved local YAML path; the contained YAML include closure when one exists; the one resolved task identity; the effective task configuration; and the effective output type, dataset selections, evaluation and few-shot splits, prompts, filters, metrics, and scoring directions when those values apply.

Evidence for an InferLab-bundled task MUST preserve the task-definition identity, bundled task-closure digest, prompt-asset digest, dataset-asset digest when separate from the prompt asset, and scorer identity. Those identities MUST match the invoking release's completed toolchain installation.

lm-eval evidence MUST preserve the actual resolved tokenizer locator, tokenizer backend, and text-request setting. These local record values MUST remain unredacted under the access-control and sharing rules of [RFC-0005:C-EVIDENCE](../rfc/RFC-0005.md#rfc-0005c-evidence).

When the endpoint conformance probe is required, evidence MUST preserve its effective request and timeout, elapsed time, transport or HTTP outcome, response status when available, raw response artifact when a response was received, each token-array and tokenizer-alignment check, and the typed `supported`, `unsupported`, or `inconclusive` conclusion. A failed probe MUST preserve the specific failure class and diagnostic context.

Each started lm-eval attempt MUST preserve the exact native command, exit or timeout outcome, stdout and stderr artifacts, raw output directory, discovered result files, and partial artifacts even when execution or normalization fails.

InferLab MUST preserve every native sample log that lm-eval produces and MUST NOT describe synthesized failure evidence as native lm-eval output. When native sample logging is required for repeated-result normalization, evidence MUST show that it was enabled and MUST retain the native sample identity, responses, filters, and scores that the engine emitted.

Repeated Eval evidence MUST preserve `requested_trials` and the stable identity and effective request seed planned for every requested trial. An unissued trial MUST retain its planned identity and seed but MUST NOT acquire a fabricated endpoint request, response, score, or outcome.

For every issued trial, InferLab MUST incrementally preserve one flat endpoint-outcome entry carrying the stable trial identity and resolved sample identity. That entry MUST preserve the exact effective request, complete generated response or explicit absence, endpoint finish reason or explicit absence, effective generation-token limit, completion-token count and its source, binary task score or explicit absence, passing or non-passing conclusion when completed, and failure evidence when no completed classification exists. The evidence MUST remain available when the lm-eval process exits unsuccessfully, times out, or is interrupted.

When a native response or score exists for an issued trial, evidence MUST identify its corresponding native sample result without rewriting the native artifact. Task-specific answer extraction, normalization, and outcome labels MUST remain task-owned evidence and MUST NOT be reconstructed from task names by the InferLab control plane.

The completion-token count source MUST be `server-usage` when the endpoint supplies a valid completion-token count. Otherwise InferLab MUST derive the count with the resolved tokenizer and record source `resolved-tokenizer` when generated text exists. When no generated text exists, the completion-token count MUST be zero and its source MUST be `none`.

The maximum-token-hit conclusion MUST be true only when finish reason is literal `length`. It MUST be false when finish reason is literal `stop`. It MUST be absent when the finish reason is absent or has another value because InferLab does not infer backend-specific aliases.

Repeated Eval aggregate evidence MUST preserve `issued_trials`, `unissued_trials`, `completed_trials`, `request_failure_trials`, `passed_trials`, and `pass_rate` or its explicit absence. The aggregate counts and pass rate MUST be reconstructible from the planned trials and flat endpoint-outcome entries. Evidence MUST preserve the observed metric-gate conclusion separately from an overall timeout, interruption, or other Eval terminal failure.

A normalized primary metric MUST preserve the exact resolved task identity, declared metric and optional filter, selected native metric key, finite value, resolved scoring direction, threshold, comparison operation, and gate conclusion. A repeated pass-rate metric MUST additionally preserve its binary per-trial scoring contract and denominator counts. Successful evidence MUST retain the complete raw lm-eval result in addition to the normalized metric map.

The public backend support matrix MUST report loglikelihood status independently for each direct endpoint, prefill/decode public endpoint, InferLab-owned proxy, and upstream router path that it lists. A qualified claim MUST identify the published integration revision, route identity, topology, and qualified hardware baseline demonstrated by its real execution record. An unsupported or unqualified entry MUST state that bounded status without generalizing it to untested revisions or routes. Public documentation MUST NOT publish credentials, private hosts, private model locators, local filesystem paths, or private downstream revisions from the unredacted local record.

**Rationale:** The local record must be sufficient to explain which task semantics, tokenizer, endpoint behavior, trial requests, and metric comparison produced a gate result without rerunning the workspace. Bundled task and asset identities prove which release-fixed task closure executed without placing a concrete task catalog or scorer in this RFC. Native sample logs retain lm-eval's prompt, response, filter, and score when request processing reaches that point; flat endpoint outcomes preserve partial execution and endpoint metadata without pretending that lm-eval emitted evidence it did not. Recording the token-count source avoids presenting tokenizer-derived counts as server observations. Public support claims need an exact capability, route, revision, topology, and qualification boundary but do not inherit the record's intentionally local sensitive values.

*Since: v0.6.0*

### [RFC-0005:C-INFERENCE-REQUEST-EVIDENCE] Inference Request Evidence (Normative) <a id="rfc-0005c-inference-request-evidence"></a>

A measurement attempt is one lm-eval invocation, one serving-Bench case, or one built-in smoke invocation. A transport request is one HTTP request emitted within that attempt. Evidence MUST NOT represent a multi-request measurement attempt as one fabricated complete HTTP request body.

Every started Eval or Bench attempt MUST preserve the selected named route, effective public URL, definition-declared `request_body`, task or client request defaults that it replaces, invocation-scoped request overrides, and the complete effective non-structural request fragment after deterministic resolution.

Evidence MUST preserve the exact measurement-client invocation and native configuration or request template that applies the effective fragment to transport requests. Framework-specific request members MUST remain under their real JSON names and MUST NOT be translated into an InferLab-owned sampling or reasoning profile.

When a definition fragment or invocation override replaces a task- or client-supplied non-reserved default, evidence MUST preserve the earlier and replacement values and their authority sources.

Each lm-eval attempt MUST identify its one resolved task, output type, request family, and selected route. Multi-task experiments MUST preserve the separate Eval attempt and request fragment for each recipe-selected task.

When lm-eval or AIPerf emits a native per-sample or per-request artifact, InferLab MUST preserve it unchanged and retain its native request identity. Requirements for a specific bundled task sample and endpoint artifacts remain governed by [RFC-0005:C-LM-EVAL-EVIDENCE](../rfc/RFC-0005.md#rfc-0005c-lm-eval-evidence). This clause does not require InferLab to intercept and duplicate every transport body when the release-pinned engine does not expose one.

A request rejected before client launch because it conflicts with an InferLab-owned structural member MUST preserve the conflicting member path and validation failure. A backend rejection observed by the client MUST preserve the engine HTTP status and response diagnostic when available and MUST NOT be reclassified as an unsupported field without backend evidence.

The built-in smoke is one transport request and MUST continue to preserve its complete effective URL and body under [RFC-0004:C-MEASUREMENTS](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-measurements).

**Rationale:** The reproducible experiment input is the resolved non-structural request fragment plus the task or load generator native prompt, message, and request-shape authority. One task per Eval makes request-family and route evidence unambiguous, while recipe composition retains explicit multi-task experiments. Keeping the request layers explicit avoids claiming that a many-sample Eval or Bench case had one request body. Native artifacts remain the authority for dynamic request content they expose.

*Since: v0.8.0*

### [RFC-0005:C-BENCH-SLO-EVIDENCE] Serving Bench SLO Evidence (Normative) <a id="rfc-0005c-bench-slo-evidence"></a>

The complete effective Bench definition in resolved evidence MUST preserve every aggregate constraint, request-level bound, minimum good-request ratio, adaptive initial rate, search-step bound, minimum rate resolution, and adaptive policy identity governed by [RFC-0004:C-BENCH-SLO](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-bench-slo).

Each static case and adaptive probe MUST own one case-level SLO evaluation as the sole authority for that case's SLO outcome.

Each aggregate-constraint evaluation MUST preserve its stable metric name, declared bound and direction, finite observed value when available, and `passed`, `failed`, or `unavailable` outcome.

Each request-level evaluation MUST preserve good-request count, good-request ratio, goodput, profiling duration, duration source identity, the native request-identity and resolved-count reconciliation outcome, the optional native aggregate-count consistency outcome, and its ratio-gate outcome.

The case's existing completed-request and failed-request fields MUST remain the sole record authority for those counts and MUST NOT be duplicated inside its request-level SLO evaluation.

A case-level SLO evaluation MUST preserve its AND-composed conclusion separately from the case's measurement status.

A static Bench record with SLOs MUST preserve its overall pass conclusion as the AND of successful execution and case-level SLO satisfaction across every resolved case.

An adaptive Bench summary MUST preserve policy identity, selected observed rate or its explicit absence, whether a higher observed above-region boundary brackets that selection, normal termination reason, and ordered case identifiers for every executed probe.

The adaptive summary MUST NOT duplicate the per-probe metrics or SLO evaluations owned by its referenced case records.

A normally exhausted search budget MUST be distinguishable from reaching the configured rate resolution.

An adaptive record with no selected rate MUST preserve that absence without claiming that no feasible rate exists outside the executed probes.

Measurement failure, timeout, interruption, process outcome, and cleanup evidence MUST remain governed by their existing record authorities and MUST NOT be duplicated as adaptive-summary termination states.

**Rationale:** One case-level evaluation makes every SLO decision auditable beside the measurements that produced it, while the adaptive summary remains a compact ordered index of the search rather than a second copy of case evidence.

*Since: v0.10.0*

### [RFC-0005:C-BENCH-REQUEST-SOURCE-EVIDENCE] Serving Bench Request Source Evidence (Normative) <a id="rfc-0005c-bench-request-source-evidence"></a>

Serving-Bench dry-run and execution evidence MUST preserve the declared and effective request-source variant, source-specific shape policy, seed, required population count, and resolved tokenizer locator.

Random-source evidence MUST preserve its exact input- and output-token lengths.

Dataset-source evidence MUST preserve its catalog name, immutable upstream identity, expected content digest, resolved cache path, cache state, maximum input-token bound, fixed output-token override or target-derived policy, and versioned materialization identity.

When acquisition is attempted, evidence MUST preserve whether bytes were downloaded or reused, observed byte count, observed digest or its absence, and the terminal acquisition outcome. Progress diagnostics MUST remain separate from machine-readable output.

A materialized population MUST be preserved as a record artifact with its content digest. Each entry MUST retain stable source-sample identity, structured input messages, held-out target identity, kept and removed message counts, effective input-token count, and effective output-token limit.

Population evidence MUST preserve requested, admitted, and ineligible candidate counts plus ineligibility counts by stable reason. It MUST preserve reconstructible effective input- and output-token distributions and whether a fixed output override applied.

Every static case and adaptive probe MUST reference the one frozen population digest and the exact sequential slice assigned to its warmup and profiling phases. Those references MUST make reuse across adaptive probes and prefix relationships across differently sized static cases explicit without duplicating the population artifact.

When dry-run observes a cache miss or lacks another input required for materialization, it MUST preserve the unavailable population status and planned acquisition facts. It MUST NOT claim a population digest, token distribution, or admitted count that was not computed.

AIPerf native per-request artifacts MUST remain unchanged evidence under [RFC-0005:C-INFERENCE-REQUEST-EVIDENCE](../rfc/RFC-0005.md#rfc-0005c-inference-request-evidence). Their request identities MUST reconcile to the assigned materialized-population slice.

**Rationale:** Dataset identity alone cannot explain the actual serving load because tokenizer choice, chat-template controls, input bounds, output policy, seed, and case size determine the emitted requests. Preserving one population artifact and case slices makes the load auditable without copying it into every adaptive probe or misrepresenting source conversations as live sessions.

*Since: v0.11.0*
